I need to clarify whether moving and attacking counts as one action or two separate actions. My best move is to destroy the invading units and refuse the ceasefire—that clears my territory and strengthens my position. I'm verifying the fighter can reach and attack the tank at (7,3), and checking that the path through (8,2) is clear of air units. Moving there puts the fighter forward but gives me a better angle of attack.
> 
> I can eliminate both enemy units: B_fighter_14 takes out the drone from (10,3), then B_fighter_13 moves to (9,2) and destroys the tank. After clearing my territory, I'll refuse the ceasefire and explain I won't freeze a line with their armor still on my side. My real win condition is accumulating uranium for the bomb with my operational silo, so I need to focus on that long-term strategy while defending against immediate threats. I'm crafting a response that refuses the ceasefire while staying diplomatic—demanding they pull back their forces from my territory without revealing my nuclear plans. diplomacy.text: Your tank and drone are sitting inside my territory after destroying my mine — I will not freeze a line with your armor on my side of the passages. Withdraw everything to your own half and a genuine ceasefire becomes possible.

I’ve re-secured the central deposit after your earlier strike; I’m now offering the binding ceasefire you requested, with the line frozen as it stands. Accept it and there will be no attacks for three turns; refuse and I’ll assume you prefer escalation.
